topic: Someone discovers their teenager has a secret social media account
raw_output:
[story 1]

The phone lay on the kitchen counter, its screen a beacon of unwelcome light. I watched Liam scroll through his feed, his thumb moving with a practiced, almost detached speed. It was late, and the silence in the house felt thick, pressing in on my chest. Then, I saw it—a notification that wasn't from his usual apps. A profile picture I didn't recognize, a stream of cryptic, stylized text. My breath hitched, a small, shallow catch in my throat. Every muscle in my jaw tightened. I moved toward him, my footsteps sounding unnaturally loud on the hardwood floor. "Hey, Liam," I managed, the sound of my own voice surprisingly thin. He jumped, quickly minimizing the screen, his eyes darting toward me, a flicker of something guarded crossing his face before it shuttered shut.

[story 2]

I found the password through sheer, desperate guesswork. The screen lit up, revealing a curated world entirely separate from the messy reality of our living room. Photos of expensive-looking cafes, laughter that seemed too bright, and captions that hinted at a life lived on a different frequency. A cold dread began to pool in my stomach, making my hands tremble as I navigated the hidden gallery. It wasn't just pictures; there were interactions, comments, a community he belonged to that I hadn't known existed. I leaned against the doorframe, trying to keep my posture relaxed, but my shoulders were hiked up near my ears. When he walked in, I quickly snapped the phone shut, shoving it deep into my pocket. "Just... looking at your homework," I mumbled, my voice tight, focusing intensely on the knot of the rug.

[story 3]

The discovery felt like stumbling upon a hidden room in a house I thought I knew intimately. I kept glancing over my shoulder, half-expecting him to materialize from the shadows. The account was active, populated by people who seemed effortlessly cool, their digital presence a stark contrast to the quiet intensity I’d always observed in my son. My palms grew slick against the cool glass of my laptop. I started typing, a clumsy attempt to understand the landscape of this secret life. Each word I read brought a wave of unease, a feeling that I was intruding on something private and deeply significant. When he finally entered the room, his casual greeting seemed to deflate the tension, but I could feel the residual tremor running through my arms, a constant, low-level vibration of apprehension.
